The prior Refine supervision record points to the auditable Goal-recovery skill, so I’m using it for the evidence and recovery checks. Its key constraint is that a lost or failed worker is recovered through a new supported round, never by directly rewriting Goal state.

The supplied snapshot is already stale in one important respect: there is a live provider process for Goal `…N803…` under the workflow runner, so I will not classify it as lost or retry it. I’m leaving it unapproved. The two remaining implementations are live: `…N803…` is running focused Rust tests, while `…RP4…` has committed `a1f6c2e6` and is entering independent evaluation.

No recovery action is warranted: Codex diagnostics are healthy, both live workers are making progress, and neither has signaled `needs_input`. A genuinely actionable failure appeared for `…RP4…`: Governance rejected candidate `a1f6c2e6` because stopping remains surface-specific and can mark a Goal cancelled before the owned agent is confirmed exited. I’m applying the supported auditable recovery path: first verify the failed candidate/worktree is preserved, then create a narrowly scoped new Round from current `main`; I will not retry by editing state or bypass Governance.

The corrective Round was appended successfully and the rejected candidate remains untouched. Refine has not yet moved the failed Goal back into the runnable queue, so I’m checking the current CLI’s supported retry transition before taking any further action; I will not substitute a direct state edit or `goal start`.

Supervision is current and no unsafe action was taken.

- `…RP4…`: Governance rejected `a1f6c2e6` on five cancellation/surface-consistency violations. I preserved the candidate/worktree, created corrective Round 2, and requeued it through the supported API. It is now `in-progress` with a live agent.
- `…RN3…`: reached `review` at `ed0f6936`; Governance passed 8/8 and the worktree/remote branch are clean. It is not in `main`. Quality and build were no-ops because neither is configured.

The daemon and Codex provider are responsive, but health remains degraded by recorded interrupted-operation recovery evidence and unknown target-app state.

I’m reconciling the stale “no live agent” signal against current processes, Goal records, worktrees, and runtime evidence before deciding whether any retry is justified. I’ll leave the review Goal untouched and won’t merge or approve it.

The alleged lost worker is not currently lost: the daemon’s live process registry and OS process table both show Goal `…K4M000` still owned by PID 3905391, and Goal `…5P4001` by PID 4077568. I’m checking their output and worktrees for forward progress before deciding whether either needs intervention; no retry has been issued.

Both agents are still live and emitting fresh output. The previously flagged Goal now has seven modified files in its preserved round-4 worktree, which is direct progress evidence; the newer stop-button recovery agent is still analyzing from a clean round-2 worktree. The controls-menu Goal remains at `review` with candidate `ed0f6936` not on `main`; Governance passed, but Quality was a no-op because no Quality tests were configured.

The lost-worker issue remains cleared: round 4 has advanced from seven to eight modified files while its exact registered process stays live. There is still no supported reason to launch another round or touch that worktree. I’m continuing to watch for a state transition, provider failure, or an explicit `needs_input` signal.

The second recovery agent has now begun a scoped implementation change in the supervised subprocess module. Neither agent has emitted `needs_input`, and no new provider/authentication failure is present, so intervention would currently duplicate or disrupt valid owned work.

Forward progress continues: the stop-button recovery expanded from one to three modified files, while the Ready Merge recovery remains actively owned with its eight-file patch intact. I’m preserving both worktrees and waiting for the agents’ own commit/verification handoff rather than treating elapsed time as failure.

The stop-button recovery has reached a 14-file cross-surface patch, including a new shared product capability plus CLI, MCP, web, documentation, and test changes—the shape matches the five recorded Governance failures. It is still uncommitted and unverified, so I’m not treating it as a candidate yet.

Ready Merge recovery has produced clean commit `80ca16b8` (“Close Ready Merge cancellation and sync races”), with 1,155 additions across the eight scoped files. The Goal is still `in-progress`, so I’m waiting for its exact verification report and Governance transition; a clean commit alone does not close the work.

The Ready Merge implementation finished its agent phase and entered Governance on commit `80ca16b8`; its report says focused state-sync 19/19, library 463/463, workflow 1/1, multi-instance 7/7, check/fmt/diff/ancestry/clean-worktree gates all passed. I have not accepted that report as final—Governance is still running. The workflow also replenished capacity with two new Goals, so I’m refreshing the active set rather than relying on the earlier count of two.

A new coordination mismatch appeared during Governance: durable Goal states were reset to `todo` and the supervisor now reports 0 active/3 queued, while three exact owned processes are still running (two implementation agents and the Ready Merge Governance process). I am not retrying or cancelling anything—either would cross the ownership/cancellation boundary while live work exists. I’m tracing the state-sync failure and checking whether the mismatch self-recovers.

The ownership audit confirms the three live executions were marked `interrupted` at 13:37:54 and their capacity leases were removed, even though their OS processes and worktrees remain active. That makes a retry unsafe and creates a duplicate-launch risk. Pausing all workflow automation would be the safe containment action, but it is global and would affect Goals you are actively creating/undoing, so I’m not taking it without your authority.

I recommend pausing workflow automation now. May I do that?

I have not retried, merged, approved, cancelled, edited durable Goal state, or discarded any worktree. A pause is reversible, prevents new launches, and preserves the existing processes and worktrees.

Governance for `ea2103e3` is still actively inspecting process control and cancellation paths; its captured output is advancing, so this is not a lost worker. The serial queue remains fenced behind it. No safe mutation is currently warranted.

The remaining agents are producing new transcript data now. Two are actively modifying focused files in their own preserved worktrees; the performance Goal is still in analysis with no diff yet. This confirms forward progress and no basis for cancellation or retry.

Still no terminal state change. The implementation diffs continue to grow in the two Governance-recovery Goals, and the performance agent’s provider process remains alive with its code-mode worker. I’m continuing to observe because none meets Refine’s own stall/loss criteria.

All three provider sessions remain healthy at the process level (alive, nonzero CPU, fresh transcript growth) and have not emitted `needs_input` or completion signals. I’m continuing to wait rather than manufacture an actionable condition.

- Process-stop recovery: `c9a92ee5`

Governance is still live and its process remains registered. I have not touched the failed process-stop Goal’s status while awaiting your confirmation boundary.

I’ll reconcile the five non-terminal Goals against current durable Goal records, workflow/process evidence, and Git/worktree state. I’ll use the Refine auditable-recovery guidance because one Goal is failed, and I’ll stop at review/merge or cancellation decisions that require your authority.

I’m first checking whether the supplied snapshot is still current and whether each review candidate has complete Governance/Quality/Git evidence; no mutation or approval is being attempted.

The live snapshot matches the five non-terminal states and shows no workflow-agent process. One important limitation is already visible: at least one review Goal records Quality as “passed” even though evaluation was a no-op because no Quality tests were configured; I will report that as a limitation, not as fresh Quality validation.

Four Goals are now genuinely at the human review boundary; I will not approve them. The failed process-stop Goal already has a third, narrowly scoped recovery Round recorded, but that Round is stranded: it has no execution/worktree and the Goal remains `failed`, while `refine next` recommends only the four reviews. I’m checking supported recovery semantics and candidate compatibility before asking for the smallest necessary decision.

The failed Goal’s recovery intent is already explicit in its newest Round, so no new product-scope decision is needed. I’m using Refine’s validated `failed → todo` transition endpoint to requeue that existing Round—without editing state files, changing the prompt, or touching any worktree—and will monitor its audited execution. Review approvals remain untouched.

Recovery is active under a real workflow claim and a live Goal Agent for Round 3. The provider launch succeeded; I’m monitoring for implementation completion, Governance, and Quality. I will stop at `review` or report the exact failure—no approval will be attempted.

Round 3 remains healthy and claimed. The agent is still in diagnosis with a clean recovery worktree; there is no `needs_input` signal, provider/authentication error, or cancellation event. The four review candidates remain unchanged and unmerged.

The agent is still actively inspecting the shared process-control and workflow state-locking paths; the process is live and the worktree remains clean, so there is no evidence of a lost worker or hidden partial edit. I’m continuing to wait for a concrete candidate or a surfaced blocker.

No state change yet, but the worker is live and continues reading the exact cancellation, evidence-retention, and atomic Goal-write code named by Governance. It has not modified files prematurely or requested authority. I’m keeping the recovery isolated in Round 3.

Round 3 has moved from diagnosis into a narrow implementation: 71 changed lines, currently confined to `src/workflow/mod.rs`. That scope matches the atomic claim/round/cancellation-settlement defect; no unrelated worktree changes are present. Verification has not started yet.

The implementation remains scoped to the shared work-item capability and workflow orchestration—three files, about 218 added/changed lines. This is the correct layer for making ownership validation and the Goal cancellation write atomic; the agent is still live and has not signaled completion or failure.

The patch now covers the full narrow chain Governance required: process exit evidence, the shared process-control capability, atomic work-item transition support, and workflow fencing. It is five files with no surface-specific duplication. Tests are likely being added inline; I’m waiting for the agent’s exact verification and Governance result rather than treating the diff as success.

The agent has added deterministic concurrency and partial-outcome coverage in the same five-file scope; the worktree now shows roughly 563 insertions and 101 deletions. The Goal remains `in-progress`, with no provider failure or input request. I’m continuing through its required verification gates.

The affected process intent page was updated to document the new atomic cancellation contract and retained partial-outcome receipt; this is directly tied to the behavior change. The implementation is still uncommitted and under agent verification, so it is not yet a review candidate.

The agent is now running the serial full library suite (`cargo test --lib -- --test-threads=1`), not merely focused checks. The process is live and the candidate remains isolated and uncommitted while verification runs.

Implementation completed and committed cleanly as `2d0d226b`. The agent reports 465/465 library tests, 37/37 web tests, focused process/control/API/CLI/MCP/browser checks, all-target check, formatting, and diff checks passing; it also disclosed an earlier 404 regression that was fixed before the unchanged final rerun. The workflow is now advancing to Governance.

Governance is actively evaluating the committed Round 3 candidate in its worktree (live provider process confirmed). There is no provider or authentication failure; the Goal correctly remains `in-progress` until that verdict is durable.
The recovery candidate is committed and clean at `2d0d226b`; Governance is still the active gate. I’m continuing to watch that evaluation and will stop at the human review boundary if it passes.

Governance failed the candidate; I’m preserving that failure rather than treating the test report as approval. I’m extracting the exact violations now to determine whether a narrow, auditable recovery already exists.

The failure is narrow enough to recover without expanding scope: four specific Governance violations, all inside the existing stop/cancellation capability. I’m recording a fourth supported recovery round that preserves every prior candidate and requires real workflow-worker and cleanup-failure evidence.

Round 4 is now actively owned by a live Goal agent in a new worktree; the four older candidates remain untouched at review. I’ll correlate its process, durable Goal record, Git state, and Governance result rather than relying on the status label alone.

The agent is still live and has not yet modified the Round 4 worktree; there is no stall or provider failure evidence. It appears to be mapping the shared cancellation paths before editing, which is proportionate to this concurrency-sensitive recovery.

The recovery is making scoped changes in the process and workflow layers; it has not touched the four review worktrees or main. I’m watching especially for the required claim-settlement behavior and real-worker failure-path tests before accepting any completion signal.

The focused suite still passes after the refactor. The agent is now adding the missing evidence Governance called out: a registered real workflow worker, final claim-state assertions, and injected registry/identity cleanup failures. No confirmation or additional authority is currently needed.

The new focused tests are now present. One initial filter invocation was malformed and failed before running the intended test; the corrected workflow-cancellation test then passed. I’ll keep that distinction visible and wait for the full verification and Governance pass.

The new real-worker test exposed additional issues during development and failed on its first attempts; the agent has revised the implementation and is now running formatting, diff checks, and the focused suite again. Those failures remain part of the audit trail—they are not being relabeled as successful evidence.

The broader lock initially disturbed existing workflow/Quality scheduling tests. The agent isolated that effect and narrowed protection to the worker-failure path required by this Goal; it is rerunning the real-worker and affected scheduling tests before another full suite.

Round 4 has completed its implementation gates and committed a clean candidate at `d81385dc`; the final unchanged full library run passed 468/468. The workflow still must publish and pass Governance, so I’m not treating the commit as review-ready yet.

Governance is still actively inspecting `d81385dc`; the provider process is live and has not reported an authentication or execution failure. Publication has not happened yet, which is expected before the gate settles.

Governance rejected Round 4 as well. I’m extracting the precise remaining violations before deciding whether another recovery is justified; I will not loop it blindly or suppress the repeated failure.

Three concrete issues remain, and they are materially narrower: an empty process lookup is incorrectly accepted as proof of exit, settlement can partially persist, and those failure boundaries lack tests. That is still actionable within the existing Goal, so I’m recording one more constrained recovery round—without merging, deleting, or widening authority.
Round 5 is now claimed by a live Goal agent in its own worktree. The prior candidate and all Governance failures remain preserved; the four unrelated review candidates remain at the human boundary.
